title: FamilyRelationships

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_5 -- "FamilyRelationships".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package -- the parent main.tex in
% this folder provides the preamble.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_5
% title: FamilyRelationships
\def\rowref{def\_3\_5}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_5}

\begin{Def}[Family relationships]
     Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes.
     We then define:
     \begin{enumerate}
         \item The set of \emph{parents} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
         %\item[] \PF{maybe better for sets (???) :The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \lp \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)\rp \sm A.\]
         %    but then  if $v \tuh v \in E$ then $v \notin \Pa^G(\lC v\rC ) \neq \Pa^G(v) \ni v$ ... }
         \item The set of \emph{children} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{siblings} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
         %\item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{ancestors} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{ancestors} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{descendants} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{descendants} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{non-descendants} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
         \item The \emph{strongly connected component} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The (union of) \emph{strongly connected components} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
         \item The \emph{district} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[\Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: } 
             v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
         \item[] The \emph{district} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.
         %\item The d-\emph{Markov blanket} of $v$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_d(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ collider walk: } 
         %    v \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus w \in G \rC \sm \{v\}.\]
         %    Note: $\lp\Pa^G(v) \cup \Ch^G(v) \cup \Pa^G(\Ch^G(v))\rp \sm \{v\} \ins \MBl^G_d(v)$.
         %\item[] The d-\emph{Markov blanket} of $A$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_d(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \MBl^G_d(v) \sm A.\]
         %\item The $\sigma$-\emph{Markov blanket} of $A$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_\sigma(A):= \MBl^{G^\acy}_d(A), \]
         %    where $G^\acy$ is an acyclification of $G$, a notion defined below in Definition~\refrow{def_3_21}.
     \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes. We then define:

  1. The set of parents of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
  2. The set of parents of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
  3. The set of children of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
  4. The set of children of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
  5. The set of siblings of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
  6. The set of ancestors of $v$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\] Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
  7. The set of ancestors of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
  8. The set of descendants of $v$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\] Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
  9. The set of descendants of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
  10. The set of non-descendants of $A$ in $G$: \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
  11. The strongly connected component of $v$ in $G$: \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\] Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
  12. The (union of) strongly connected components of $A$ in $G$: \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
  13. The district of $v$ in $G$: \[\Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: } v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \rC.\] Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
  14. The district of $A$ in $G$: \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\] Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.

lean_code_with_comments:
/-- `Pa G v` -- the set of *parents* of the vertex `v` in `G`:
those `w ∈ G` with a directed edge `w ⟶[G] v`. Matches the LN's
`\Pa^G(v)` (`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`, def 3.5
item 1). Parents can lie in either `G.J` or `G.V`. -/
def Pa (G : CDMG α) (v : α) : Set α :=
  {w | w ∈ G ∧ w ⟶[G] v}

/-- `PaSet G A` -- the set of *parents* of the set of vertices
`A` in `G`: the union of the parent sets of each `v ∈ A`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Pa^G(A)`. -/
def PaSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Pa G v

/-- `Ch G v` -- the set of *children* of the vertex `v` in `G`:
those `w ∈ G` with a directed edge `v ⟶[G] w`. Matches the LN's
`\Ch^G(v)`. Children are always output nodes (`w ∈ G.V` follows
from `G.E_subset`); inputs cannot be children. -/
def Ch (G : CDMG α) (v : α) : Set α :=
  {w | w ∈ G ∧ v ⟶[G] w}

/-- `ChSet G A` -- the set of *children* of the set of vertices
`A` in `G`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Ch^G(A)`. -/
def ChSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Ch G v

/-- `Sib G v` -- the set of *siblings* of the vertex `v` in `G`:
those `w ∈ G` with a bidirected edge `v ⟷[G] w`. Matches the LN's
`\Sib^G(v)`. The LN intentionally provides no set-input
counterpart, and neither do we. -/
def Sib (G : CDMG α) (v : α) : Set α :=
  {w | w ∈ G ∧ v ⟷[G] w}

/-- `Anc G v` -- the set of *ancestors* of the vertex `v` in `G`:
those `w ∈ G` for which there exists a *directed walk* (in the
def_3_4 sense, i.e. `Walk G w v` plus `Walk.IsDirected`) from
`w` to `v`. Matches the LN's `\Anc^G(v)`. -/
def Anc (G : CDMG α) (v : α) : Set α :=
  {w | w ∈ G ∧ ∃ π : Walk G w v, π.IsDirected}

/-- LN "Note: `v ∈ Anc^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 4). The witness is
the trivial walk `Walk.nil v`. The `v ∈ G` hypothesis is the
LN's implicit precondition (its preamble fixes `v ∈ V ⊆ G`). -/
theorem self_mem_Anc {G : CDMG α} {v : α} (hv : v ∈ G) :
    v ∈ Anc G v :=
  ⟨hv, Walk.nil v, by simp⟩

/-- `AncSet G A` -- the set of *ancestors* of the set `A` in `G`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Anc^G(A)`. -/
def AncSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Anc G v

/-- LN "Note: `A ⊆ Anc^G(A)`" (def 3.5, item 4). Each
`v ∈ A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` is its own ancestor via the trivial walk. -/
theorem subset_Anc_set {G : CDMG α} {A : Set α}
    (hA : A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : A ⊆ AncSet G A := by
  intro v hvA
  rw [mem_AncSet]
  exact ⟨v, hvA, self_mem_Anc (hA hvA)⟩

/-- `Desc G v` -- the set of *descendants* of the vertex `v` in
`G`: those `w ∈ G` for which there exists a directed walk from
`v` to `w`. Matches the LN's `\Desc^G(v)`. -/
def Desc (G : CDMG α) (v : α) : Set α :=
  {w | w ∈ G ∧ ∃ π : Walk G v w, π.IsDirected}

/-- LN "Note: `v ∈ Desc^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 5). Witness:
trivial walk. -/
theorem self_mem_Desc {G : CDMG α} {v : α} (hv : v ∈ G) :
    v ∈ Desc G v :=
  ⟨hv, Walk.nil v, by simp⟩

/-- `DescSet G A` -- the set of *descendants* of the set `A` in
`G`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Desc^G(A)`. -/
def DescSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Desc G v

/-- LN "Note: `A ⊆ Desc^G(A)`" (def 3.5, item 5). -/
theorem subset_Desc_set {G : CDMG α} {A : Set α}
    (hA : A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : A ⊆ DescSet G A := by
  intro v hvA
  rw [mem_DescSet]
  exact ⟨v, hvA, self_mem_Desc (hA hvA)⟩

/-- `NonDesc G A` -- the set of *non-descendants* of the set `A`
in `G`: vertices of `G` (i.e. members of `G.J ∪ G.V`) that are
not descendants of any vertex in `A`. Matches the LN's
`\NonDesc^G(A)`. The LN provides no vertex-input variant. -/
def NonDesc (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  (G.J ∪ G.V) \ DescSet G A

/-- `Sc G v` -- the *strongly connected component* of `v` in `G`:
the intersection of the ancestor and descendant sets of `v`.
Matches the LN's `\Sc^G(v)`. Equivalently, the set of `w ∈ G`
that lie on a directed walk to `v` and on a directed walk from
`v`. -/
def Sc (G : CDMG α) (v : α) : Set α :=
  Anc G v ∩ Desc G v

/-- LN "Note: `v ∈ Sc^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 7). Composes
`self_mem_Anc` and `self_mem_Desc`. -/
theorem self_mem_Sc {G : CDMG α} {v : α} (hv : v ∈ G) :
    v ∈ Sc G v :=
  ⟨self_mem_Anc hv, self_mem_Desc hv⟩

/-- `ScSet G A` -- the union of *strongly connected components*
of vertices in `A`. Matches the LN's set-input `\Sc^G(A)`. -/
def ScSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Sc G v

/-- LN "Note: `A ⊆ Sc^G(A)`" (def 3.5, item 7). -/
theorem subset_Sc_set {G : CDMG α} {A : Set α}
    (hA : A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : A ⊆ ScSet G A := by
  intro v hvA
  rw [mem_ScSet]
  exact ⟨v, hvA, self_mem_Sc (hA hvA)⟩

/-- `Dist G v` -- the *district* of `v` in `G`: those `w ∈ G` for
which there exists a *bidirected walk* (every step is `bidir`)
from `v` to `w`. Matches the LN's `\Dist^G(v)`. -/
def Dist (G : CDMG α) (v : α) : Set α :=
  {w | w ∈ G ∧ ∃ π : Walk G v w, π.IsBidirected}

/-- LN "Note: `v ∈ Dist^G(v)`" (def 3.5, item 8). Witness:
trivial walk, which is vacuously `IsBidirected`. -/
theorem self_mem_Dist {G : CDMG α} {v : α} (hv : v ∈ G) :
    v ∈ Dist G v :=
  ⟨hv, Walk.nil v, by simp⟩

/-- `DistSet G A` -- the *district* of the set `A` in `G`.
Matches the LN's set-input `\Dist^G(A)`. -/
def DistSet (G : CDMG α) (A : Set α) : Set α :=
  ⋃ v ∈ A, Dist G v

/-- LN "Note: `A ⊆ Dist^G(A)`" (def 3.5, item 8). -/
theorem subset_Dist_set {G : CDMG α} {A : Set α}
    (hA : A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : A ⊆ DistSet G A := by
  intro v hvA
  rw [mem_DistSet]
  exact ⟨v, hvA, self_mem_Dist (hA hvA)⟩
lean_explanation:
The Lean encoding maps the LN's eight family-relationship operators of def 3.5 to a uniform collection of `Set α`-valued definitions, split across three sibling files (`FamilyDirect.lean`, `FamilyReachability.lean`, `FamilyDistrict.lean`). The ambient `α : Type*` is the vertex carrier and `G : CDMG α` is an explicit graph parameter — chapter 5's intervention $G^{do(v)}$ asks for $\Pa^{G^{do(v)}}(v)$ on a different CDMG, so `G` cannot sit in `variable`. The directed-edge atom $w \tuh v$ from def 3.2 is written `w ⟶[G] v` (notation for `(w, v) ∈ G.E`), and the bidirected atom $v \huh w$ is `v ⟷[G] w` (notation for `G.L v w`). For reachability operators the walk infrastructure of def 3.4 is reused wholesale: `Walk G w v` is the inductive walk data type, and `Walk.IsDirected` / `Walk.IsBidirected` are the predicates pinning each step's kind.

Each LN operator becomes a `Set α`-valued `def`, expressed as `{w | w ∈ G ∧ ⋯}` to mirror the LN's literal $\{w \in G \mid \cdot\}$ text. The set-input variant (e.g. $\Pa^G(A)$) is always `⋃ v ∈ A, f G v`, the Mathlib `Set.iUnion₂` idiom — `⋃ v ∈ A, _` is shorthand for an iterated `Set.iUnion`, and its membership lemma `Set.mem_iUnion` composed twice unfolds to the existential $\exists v \in A, \ldots$ that downstream proofs want. Each `def` is paired with an `@[simp]` membership lemma (`mem_Pa`, `mem_AncSet`, …) that makes downstream rewrites uniform.

The existential "$\exists$ directed walk" of items 4, 5, 7, 8 is rendered as `∃ π : Walk G w v, π.IsDirected` (for ancestors; the walk direction flips for descendants, and the predicate becomes `IsBidirected` for districts). The non-descendant operator `NonDesc G A` is `(G.J ∪ G.V) \ DescSet G A` — set difference inside the ambient $J \cup V$ rather than `Set.univ`, since a vertex of type `α` that doesn't lie in `G` is not a non-descendant; it simply isn't a vertex of the graph at all. Finally, every LN "Note: $v \in \cdot$" / "Note: $A \subseteq \cdot$" reflexivity line surfaces as a named Lean `theorem` (`self_mem_Anc`, `subset_Anc_set`, `self_mem_Desc`, `subset_Desc_set`, `self_mem_Sc`, `subset_Sc_set`, `self_mem_Dist`, `subset_Dist_set`), each carrying the LN's silent preamble assumption ($v \in V$ or $A \subseteq J \cup V$) as an explicit `v ∈ G` / `A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` hypothesis. The witness is the trivial walk `Walk.nil v`, vacuously both `IsDirected` and `IsBidirected`.
design_choices:
**Three sibling files, split by underlying primitive.** The eight operators of def 3.5 partition cleanly by what they rest on: items 1–3 (parents, children, siblings) use only the single-edge atoms of def 3.2; items 4–7 (ancestors, descendants, non-descendants, strongly connected component) sit on def 3.4's directed walks; item 8 (district) sits on bidirected walks. We split accordingly into `FamilyDirect.lean`, `FamilyReachability.lean`, and `FamilyDistrict.lean`; none imports the others, so a row that only needs $\Pa^G$ never accidentally pulls in the full walk infrastructure.

**`Set α`-valued, parameterised by an explicit `G : CDMG α`.** The LN literally writes set-builders, so a `Set α`-valued `def` is the natural codomain. The graph `G` is an explicit function argument rather than a `variable`, because chapter 5's intervention $G^{do(v)}$ asks for $\Pa^{G^{do(v)}}(v)$ on a *different* CDMG.

**`w ∈ G` guard inside every comprehension.** Each body reads `{w | w ∈ G ∧ ⋯}`, matching the LN's literal $\{w \in G \mid \cdot\}$. For the direct-edge operators (Pa, Ch, Sib) the guard is propositionally redundant — `G.E_subset` / `G.L_subset` already place endpoints in $J \cup V$ — but for the walk-based operators it is *load-bearing*: the trivial walk `Walk.nil v` is vacuously both directed and bidirected, so without the guard every $v : \alpha$ (including vertices outside the graph) would land in $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, and $\Dist^G(v)$. We keep the guard uniformly so callers see the same membership shape across all eight operators.

**Two `def`s per operator, except where the LN doesn't.** Each operator has a vertex variant (`Pa : CDMG α → α → Set α`) and a set variant suffixed `Set` (`PaSet : CDMG α → Set α → Set α`). The LN overloads $\Pa^G(\cdot)$ on both inputs; Lean cannot, so we disambiguate by suffix. We mirror the LN's two intentional omissions exactly: no `SibSet` (the LN gives no $\Sib^G(A)$) and no vertex `NonDesc` (the LN gives no $\NonDesc^G(v)$). The set variants are always literally `⋃ v ∈ A, f G v` — declaring an independent set-level primitive would invite a "the two agree" lemma we'd rather not need.

**Walk-existentials over the existing `Walk` data, not fresh inductives.** The reachability operators (`Anc`, `Desc`, `Sc`, `Dist`) are encoded as `∃ π : Walk G w v, π.IsDirected` (or `IsBidirected`), reusing the entire walk API (`length`, `support`, `append`, `reverse`) which downstream rows (claim 3.2, acyclicity, do-calculus) need anyway. A separate inductive ancestor / descendant relation would force re-proving all of that infrastructure; existential phrasing on top of `Walk` is essentially free.

**`NonDesc^G(A)` complements inside $J \cup V$, not $\alpha$.** The LN writes $(J \cup V) \setminus \Desc^G(A)$, not $\alpha \setminus \Desc^G(A)$. A vertex outside `G` is not a non-descendant — it isn't a vertex of the graph at all. This makes `NonDesc G A ⊆ G` automatic, dovetailing with the `w ∈ G` guard the other operators bake in.

**Reflexivity "Note:" lines as named theorems with explicit preconditions.** Each LN "Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$" / "Note: $A \subseteq \Anc^G(A)$" becomes a Lean `theorem` (`self_mem_Anc`, `subset_Anc_set`, …) rather than being left implicit. The LN's silent "$v \in V$" / "$A \subseteq J \cup V$" preamble assumption is recorded as an explicit `v ∈ G` / `A ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` hypothesis — without it the reflexivity is false for vertices outside the graph, since the `w ∈ G` guard rules them out. The witness in every case is the trivial walk `Walk.nil v`, vacuously both `IsDirected` and `IsBidirected`.
